messages.cluster.wahlrecht.title: Wahlrecht — Elections cheat sheet
messages.cluster.wahlrecht.body:
• German elections are: frei (free), gleich (equal), geheim (secret) — and allgemein (universal), unmittelbar (direct).
• Bundestag = federal parliament, elected every 4 years.
• Landesparlament (incl. Berlin Abgeordnetenhaus) = elected every 5 years.
• 5%-Hürde: a party needs ≥5% of second votes to enter the Bundestag.
• Aktives Wahlrecht = right to vote (≥18). Passives Wahlrecht = right to be elected.
• Wahlhelfer count votes after polls close (it's an Ehrenamt, a volunteer role).
• Foreigners can vote in some Kommunalwahlen (EU citizens only); not in Bundestag elections.
messages.cluster.grundrechte.title: Grundgesetz & Grundrechte — Basic Law cheat sheet
messages.cluster.grundrechte.body:
• Grundgesetz = Germany's constitution (Verfassung). Article 1: 'Die Würde des Menschen ist unantastbar' — human dignity is inviolable.
• Core Grundrechte: Meinungsfreiheit (opinion), Pressefreiheit (press), Religionsfreiheit (religion), Versammlungsfreiheit (assembly), Gleichberechtigung (equality).
• A Grundrecht cannot be abolished — even by Bundestag majority.
• Rechtsstaat = rule of law: state AND all residents follow the laws.
• Todesstrafe (death penalty), Folter (torture), Zwangsarbeit (forced labor), Zwangsheirat (forced marriage) — all verboten.
• Religious instruction in school is parents' choice (until child turns 14).
messages.cluster.nszeit.title: NS-Zeit, WWII, DDR — History cheat sheet
messages.cluster.nszeit.body:
• 1933 = Nazis (Nationalsozialisten) come to power. 1945 = end of WWII / Nazi rule. 8. Mai 1945 = end of war in Europe.
• Holocaust: systematic murder of European Jews. Germany has special responsibility toward Israel because of these Verbrechen.
• 1949 = BRD (Bundesrepublik) and DDR founded. Grundgesetz takes effect.
• 4 Besatzungszonen after WWII: USA, UK, France, Sowjetunion.
• 1961 = Berliner Mauer built. 1989 = Mauerfall (fall). 1990 (3. Oktober) = Wiedervereinigung — German Unity Day.
• 5 'neue Bundesländer' from former DDR: Brandenburg, Mecklenburg-Vorpommern, Sachsen, Sachsen-Anhalt, Thüringen.
• 50er Jahre = Wirtschaftswunder. First Gastarbeiter came from Italy (1955).
messages.cluster.berlin.title: Berlin — State deck cheat sheet
messages.cluster.berlin.body:
• Berlin is a Stadtstaat (city-state) and one of 16 Bundesländer.
• Coat of arms (Wappen): black bear on white shield with red crown.
• Flagge: weiß-rot (white-red) with the bear.
• Berlin has 12 Bezirke (e.g. Pankow, Mitte, Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf).
• Government = Senat. Head = Regierende(r) Bürgermeister(in) (NOT Ministerpräsident).
• Abgeordnetenhaus (state parliament) elected every 5 years.
• Wahlalter for Abgeordnetenhaus: 16. Wahlalter for Bundestag: 18.
• Senats roles cover Bildung, Inneres, Justiz, Verkehr… NOT Außenbeziehungen (foreign affairs belong to the Bund).
messages.glossary.1933.context: Nazis (Nationalsozialisten) take power. End of the Weimar Republic.
messages.glossary.1945.context: End of WWII / Nazi rule. 8. Mai 1945 = end of war in Europe.
messages.glossary.1989.context: Fall of the Berliner Mauer (9. November). Beginning of Wiedervereinigung.
messages.glossary.1990.context: German reunification (3. Oktober) — now German Unity Day (Tag der Deutschen Einheit).
messages.glossary.Wahl.context: An election. German elections must be frei, gleich, geheim — free, equal, secret.
messages.glossary.Wahlen.context: Elections. Bundestag every 4 years; Landtag every 5; Kommunalwahlen at municipal level.
messages.glossary.nicht.context: Negation — flips the question. Always read negated questions twice before picking.
messages.glossary.Bundestag.context: The federal parliament. Elected every 4 years; sits in Berlin (Reichstag building).
messages.glossary.DDR.context: East Germany (Deutsche Demokratische Republik), 1949–1990. A dictatorship led by the SED.
messages.glossary.kann.context: 'Can/may'. Often signals a choice or possibility — read whether the question asks what is allowed.
messages.glossary.Berlin.context: The capital city of Germany and a Stadtstaat (city-state) — one of 16 Bundesländer.
messages.glossary.Gesetz.context: A law. Passed by the Bundestag (and where relevant, the Bundesrat).
messages.glossary.Partei.context: A political party. Needs ≥5% of second votes to enter the Bundestag.
messages.glossary.alle.context: 'All/every'. Watch for traps: 'all immigrants must…' usually wrong; 'all residents…' often right.
messages.glossary.Bundesland.context: A federal state. Germany has 16 — including Berlin, Hamburg, Bremen as Stadtstaaten.
messages.glossary.darf.context: 'May / is allowed'. Marks a question about permission or right.
messages.glossary.Gericht.context: A court. Independent — judges (Richter) decide based on Gesetz, not government.
messages.glossary.Bundeskanzler.context: The Chancellor — head of the federal government. Elected by the Bundestag.
messages.glossary.Grundgesetz.context: The Basic Law — Germany's constitution. Adopted 1949. Article 1 = human dignity.
messages.glossary.muss.context: 'Must'. A strict requirement — careful: 'every citizen MUST vote' is wrong (it's a right).
messages.glossary.Gesetze.context: Laws (plural). Made by the Bundestag; the state and all residents must follow them.
messages.glossary.Verfassung.context: Constitution. In Germany, the Grundgesetz fills this role.
messages.glossary.Richter.context: A judge. Independent of government. Some are honorary (ehrenamtliche Richter/Schöffen).
messages.glossary.kein.context: 'No / not any'. A negation — flips what the question is asking for.
messages.glossary.nur.context: 'Only'. Almost always a trap word — 'only X' is rarely the correct answer.
messages.glossary.Bundespräsident.context: The Federal President — Staatsoberhaupt. Largely ceremonial. Elected by Bundesversammlung.
messages.glossary.Bundespräsidentin.context: The Federal President (female form). Same role as Bundespräsident.
messages.glossary.Sozialversicherung.context: Statutory social insurance: health, pension, unemployment, accident, long-term care.
messages.glossary.Bundeskanzlerin.context: The Chancellor (female form). Head of the federal government.
messages.glossary.Mauer.context: Wall — specifically the Berliner Mauer (1961–1989) that split East and West Berlin.
messages.glossary.Nationalsozialisten.context: The Nazis — came to power 1933 under Hitler. Ended 1945.
messages.glossary.Meinungsfreiheit.context: Freedom of opinion — a Grundrecht. You may publicly criticize the government.
messages.glossary.keine.context: 'No / not any' (feminine form). A negation that flips the question.
messages.glossary.Wappen.context: Coat of arms. Berlin's = bear on white shield with red crown.
messages.glossary.Demokratie.context: Democracy. Germany is a representative, federal, social democracy.
messages.glossary.Fraktion.context: A parliamentary group — MPs of the same party (or aligned parties) acting together.
messages.glossary.Bundesregierung.context: The federal government — Chancellor plus federal ministers.
messages.glossary.Bundesrat.context: The Bundesrat (Federal Council) represents the 16 Bundesländer at federal level.
messages.glossary.Ehrenamt.context: Volunteer / honorary work, unpaid. E.g. Wahlhelfer, ehrenamtliche Richter.
messages.ui.answerVariantNote: May appear worded slightly differently on the real exam.
messages.questions.1.answers.0.why: Religionsfreiheit exists, but it covers religion, not government criticism.
messages.questions.1.answers.1.why: Paying taxes doesn't grant the right to criticise government.
messages.questions.1.answers.2.why: The right to vote is separate from the right to criticise.
messages.questions.1.answers.3.why: Correct — Meinungsfreiheit is the right at stake.
messages.questions.2.answers.1.why: Correct — Religion is the only subject parents can opt out of.
messages.questions.3.answers.0.why: Correct — Rechtsstaat = everyone, including the state, follows the law.
messages.questions.3.answers.1.why: Wrong — in a Rechtsstaat the state IS bound by law.
messages.questions.3.answers.2.why: Wrong — laws apply to ALL residents, not only Germans.
messages.questions.3.answers.3.why: Wrong — laws are made by the Bundestag, not by the courts.
messages.questions.4.answers.0.why: Owning weapons is restricted, not a Grundrecht.
messages.questions.4.answers.1.why: 'Right of the fist' (Faustrecht) is the opposite of rule of law.
messages.questions.4.answers.2.why: Correct — Meinungsfreiheit is a core Grundrecht (Art. 5).
messages.questions.5.answers.0.why: Wrong — accepting money to vote a certain way is bribery, the opposite of 'frei'.
messages.questions.5.answers.1.why: Wrong — excluding ex-prisoners isn't what 'frei' means.
messages.questions.5.answers.2.why: Correct — 'frei' = no coercion, no influence, no disadvantage from the vote.
messages.questions.5.answers.3.why: Wrong — compulsory voting (Wahlpflicht) is the opposite of 'frei'.
messages.questions.21.imageCaption: Germany's Bundesflagge is three horizontal stripes: schwarz-rot-gold (black-red-gold), top to bottom.
messages.questions.25.answers.0.why: Correct — Elsass-Lothringen is in France today (Alsace-Lorraine), NOT a Bundesland.
messages.questions.25.answers.1.why: Wrong — Nordrhein-Westfalen IS a Bundesland (the most populous).
messages.questions.25.answers.2.why: Wrong — Mecklenburg-Vorpommern IS a Bundesland (north-east coast).
messages.questions.25.answers.3.why: Wrong — Sachsen-Anhalt IS a Bundesland (one of the 'neue Länder').
messages.questions.30.answers.0.why: Wrong — regelmäßige Wahlen (regular elections) ARE a feature of democracy.
messages.questions.30.answers.1.why: Correct — Pressezensur (press censorship) does NOT belong in a democracy.
messages.questions.30.answers.2.why: Wrong — Meinungsfreiheit (freedom of opinion) IS a core feature of democracy.
messages.questions.30.answers.3.why: Wrong — multiple parties (Mehrparteiensystem) ARE a feature of democracy.

messages.questions.75.answers.0.why: Correct — Frank-Walter Steinmeier has been Bundespräsident since 2017.
messages.questions.75.answers.1.why: Wrong — Bärbel Bas was Bundestagspräsidentin (head of parliament), not Staatsoberhaupt.
messages.questions.75.answers.2.why: Wrong — Bodo Ramelow was Ministerpräsident of Thüringen, not Staatsoberhaupt.
messages.questions.75.answers.3.why: Wrong — Joachim Gauck was the previous Bundespräsident (2012–2017), but Steinmeier holds the office now.
messages.questions.100.answers.0.why: Correct — Lebensversicherung (life insurance) is PRIVATE, not statutory.
messages.questions.100.answers.1.why: Renten- (pension) is one of the 5 statutory pillars.
messages.questions.104.answers.0.why: Long illness CAN be grounds for dismissal (with strict rules).
messages.questions.104.answers.1.why: Being late repeatedly CAN justify dismissal.
messages.questions.104.answers.2.why: Private affairs at work CAN justify dismissal.
messages.questions.104.answers.3.why: Correct — pregnancy is protected; firing for pregnancy is illegal (Mutterschutzgesetz).
messages.questions.107.answers.0.why: Too short — 2 years is not the Wahlperiode.
messages.questions.107.answers.2.why: Wrong — 6 years is the term in some countries, not Germany.
messages.questions.107.answers.3.why: Wrong — 8 years is nowhere in German politics.
messages.questions.110.answers.3.why: That's the Landesparlament term length, not Bundestag.

messages.questions.152.answers.0.why: Wrong — 1918–1923 is the early Weimar Republic, not the Nazi era.
messages.questions.152.answers.1.why: Wrong — 1932–1950 misses both endpoints; Nazis came to power in 1933, not 1932.
messages.questions.152.answers.2.why: Correct — Hitler and the Nazis ruled 1933 to 1945.
messages.questions.152.answers.3.why: Wrong — 1945–1989 covers post-war Germany (BRD/DDR + Wall era), not the Nazi era.
messages.questions.155.answers.0.why: Wrong — 1888–1918 is the Kaiserreich, not the Nazi era.
messages.questions.155.answers.1.why: Wrong — Nazis came to power in 1933, not 1921.
messages.questions.155.answers.3.why: Wrong — 1949–1963 is Adenauer's early BRD years, not the Nazi era.

messages.questions.217.answers.1.why: Wrong — 1933–1945 is the Nazi era, before the DDR existed.
messages.questions.217.answers.2.why: Wrong — 1945–1961 is post-war division, but the DDR was founded in 1949, not 1945.

messages.questions.301.answers.0.why: Wrong Wappen — that's not the Berlin bear shield.
messages.questions.301.answers.3.why: Correct — Berlin's Wappen: black bear on white shield with red crown.
messages.questions.302.answers.0.why: Altona is a Hamburg district — NOT a Berlin Bezirk.
messages.questions.302.answers.1.why: Prignitz is a Brandenburg district — NOT Berlin.
messages.questions.302.answers.2.why: Correct — Pankow is one of Berlin's 12 Bezirke.
messages.questions.302.answers.3.why: Mecklenburgische Seenplatte is in Mecklenburg-Vorpommern — NOT Berlin.
messages.questions.303.answers.1.why: That's the Bundestag term, not the Landesparlament.
messages.questions.304.answers.0.why: Too low — 14 is below Wahlalter anywhere in Germany.
messages.questions.304.answers.2.why: That's the Bundestag voting age, not Berlin's Kommunalwahl.
messages.questions.305.answers.1.why: Correct — Berlin's Landesflagge is weiß-rot (white-red) with the bear.
messages.questions.305.answers.3.why: Wrong — black-gold is not Berlin's flag.
messages.questions.306.answers.0.why: Ordnungsamt handles public order, not political education.
messages.questions.306.answers.1.why: Churches are about faith, not political education.
messages.questions.306.answers.2.why: Verbraucherzentrale is for consumer rights, not political education.
messages.questions.306.answers.3.why: Correct — Landeszentrale für politische Bildung handles civic education.
messages.questions.307.answers.0.why: Correct — Berlin is one of 3 Stadtstaaten (with Hamburg and Bremen).
messages.questions.307.answers.1.why: Saarland is a Flächenstaat in the southwest.
messages.questions.307.answers.2.why: Brandenburg is a Flächenstaat that surrounds Berlin.

messages.questions.309.answers.0.why: Ministerpräsident(in) is the title in Flächenstaaten, NOT in Berlin.
messages.questions.309.answers.1.why: Oberbürgermeister(in) is a city-mayor title (e.g. Köln, München) — not Berlin's state government head.
messages.questions.309.answers.2.why: Präsident des Senats is Bremen's title, NOT Berlin's.
messages.questions.310.answers.2.why: Correct — there's NO 'Senator für Außenbeziehungen' (foreign affairs is a Bund matter).
